_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to hear and play with the sounds in spoken words — syllables, rhymes and individual sounds.

_source.w: We are learning to hear and play with the sounds in spoken words — syllables, rhymes and individual sounds.
y: Hearing the sounds in words is the foundation of learning to read and write.
Playing with sounds helps our brain get ready to connect spoken words with written letters.
The better we hear sounds, the easier it will be to spell and decode words.
eg: We clap out the syllables in our names — 'Ma-ri-a' gets three claps.
We listen to two words and decide if they rhyme — like 'cat' and 'hat'.
We think of words that start with the same sound — like 'sun', 'sand' and 'sing'.
We play a game where we change one sound in a word to make a new word — 'cat' becomes 'bat'.
We listen for the odd one out — which word does not rhyme with the others?
We generate our own rhyming words after hearing a pattern — 'dog, log, fog…'
